section.title: The Tabernacle
section.sectionRationale: Having described the furnishings that will fill it, God now gives Moses the pattern for the tabernacle structure itself: its linen and goat hair coverings, its acacia wood frames, and the veil that will separate the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place.
section.thesis: God instructs Moses to build the tabernacle in layers, from its inner curtains of linen and cherubim to its outer coverings and standing frames, and to hang a veil that separates the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place where the ark rests.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the inner linen curtains woven with cherubim; the outer coverings of goat hair, ram skins, and fine leather; the acacia wood frames that give the tent its shape; and the veil that divides the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place.

media.alt: Linen curtains joined with gold clasps
media.caption: Ten curtains of linen and blue, purple, and scarlet yarn are joined into a single tabernacle.

verses.text: “You are to construct the {{tabernacle|tabernacle}} itself with ten curtains of finely spun linen, each with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and cherubim skillfully worked into them.
Each curtain shall be twenty-eight cubits long and four cubits wide, all curtains the same size.
Five of the curtains are to be joined together, and the other five joined as well.
Make loops of blue material on the edge of the end curtain in the first set, and do the same for the end curtain in the second set.
Make fifty loops on one curtain and fifty loops on the end curtain of the second set, so that the loops line up opposite one another.
Make fifty gold clasps as well, and join the curtains together with the clasps, so that the tabernacle will be a unit.

units.label: The outer coverings
units.note: goat hair, ram skins, fine leather

verses.text: You are to make curtains of goat hair for the tent over the tabernacle, eleven curtains in all.
Each of the eleven curtains is to be the same size, thirty cubits long and four cubits wide.
Join five of the curtains into one set and the other six into another. Then fold the sixth curtain over double at the front of the tent.
Make fifty loops along the edge of the end curtain in the first set, and fifty loops along the edge of the corresponding curtain in the second set.
Make fifty bronze clasps and put them through the loops to join the tent together as a unit.
As for the overlap that remains of the tent curtains, the half curtain that is left over shall hang down over the back of the tabernacle.
And the tent curtains will be a cubit longer on either side, and the excess will hang over the sides of the tabernacle to cover it.
Also make a covering for the tent out of ram skins dyed red, and over that a covering of fine leather.

units.label: The upright frames
units.note: according to the pattern shown you on the mountain

verses.text: You are to construct upright frames of acacia wood for the tabernacle.
Each frame is to be ten cubits long and a cubit and a half wide.
Two tenons must be connected to each other for each frame. Make all the frames of the tabernacle in this way.
Construct twenty frames for the south side of the tabernacle,
with forty silver bases under the twenty frames, two bases for each frame, one under each tenon.
For the second side of the tabernacle, the north side, make twenty frames
and forty silver bases, two bases under each frame.
Make six frames for the rear of the tabernacle, the west side,
and two frames for the two back corners of the tabernacle,
coupled together from bottom to top and fitted into a single ring. These will serve as the two corners.
So there are to be eight frames and sixteen silver bases, two under each frame.
You are also to make five crossbars of acacia wood for the frames on one side of the tabernacle,
five for those on the other side, and five for those on the rear side of the tabernacle, to the west.
The central crossbar in the middle of the frames shall extend from one end to the other.
Overlay the frames with gold and make gold rings to hold the crossbars. Also overlay the crossbars with gold.
So you are to set up the tabernacle according to the pattern shown you on the mountain.

units.label: The veil and the screen
units.note: separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place

media.alt: A woven veil hung on gold hooks before a hidden ark
media.caption: The veil shields the ark of the Testimony behind it, separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place.

verses.text: Make a {{veil|veil}} of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely spun linen, with cherubim skillfully worked into it.
Hang it with gold hooks on four posts of acacia wood, overlaid with gold and standing on four silver bases.
And hang the veil from the clasps and place the ark of the Testimony behind the veil. So the veil will separate the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place.
Put the mercy seat on the ark of the Testimony in the Most Holy Place.
And place the table outside the veil on the north side of the tabernacle, and put the lampstand opposite the table, on the south side.
For the entrance to the tent, you are to make a curtain embroidered with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and finely spun linen.
Make five posts of acacia wood for the curtain, overlay them with gold hooks, and cast five bronze bases for them.

tabernacle.gloss: Dwelling place, from the same root as the verb to dwell. The tabernacle is literally God's tent, a portable dwelling that travels with Israel through the wilderness, always among His people rather than fixed in one distant place.

veil.gloss: The inner veil, a heavy woven curtain separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place where the ark rested. It marked how restricted access to God's immediate presence was under the old covenant, entered only once a year by the high priest.

basic.context: After describing the furnishings, God now gives the pattern for the tabernacle itself: layers of curtains, a wooden frame structure, and a veil separating the Holy Place from the innermost Most Holy Place where the ark sat. Every layer moves from the most costly and beautiful materials closest to God's presence toward plainer materials on the outside.
basic.hermeneutics: These are literal, practical building instructions for Israel's actual portable sanctuary, not primarily a code to decode. The structure itself communicates something true without needing every measurement to carry separate symbolism: increasing holiness the closer one moves toward the ark, and real, careful separation between a holy God and sinful people.
basic.originalLanguages: The mishkan, dwelling place, is God's tent, moving with His people rather than remaining fixed. The parokhet, veil, woven with cherubim like the ones guarding Eden, keeps the way to the Most Holy Place shut, a visible, physical reminder that access to God's presence remains restricted under this covenant.
basic.typology: The veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place points forward to the barrier sin creates between people and God, a barrier torn in two at the moment of Christ's death, Matthew 27:51. The tabernacle itself, God's dwelling among His people, anticipates the incarnation, when the Word became flesh and dwelt among us.
basic.christ: John says the Word became flesh and 'tabernacled' among us, John 1:14, using the very language of this structure to describe Jesus. When Jesus died, the temple veil was torn from top to bottom, showing that the separation this tabernacle maintained has been decisively ended in Him, Hebrews 10:19-20.
basic.god: God's holiness required careful, deliberate separation, layer after layer, from ordinary people, yet His whole purpose was still to dwell among them. Even in the most restrictive structure, His desire for nearness to His people is unmistakable.
basic.crossReferences: John 1:14 says the Word tabernacled among us. Matthew 27:51 records the temple veil torn in two at Christ's death. Hebrews 9:1-8 explains the two chambers and their meaning. Hebrews 10:19-20 says believers now have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place through Christ's flesh.
basic.oneStory: God gives detailed instructions for a dwelling that keeps His holy presence carefully separated from sinful people by curtain after curtain and a final veil. That separation is exactly what Christ ends: He tabernacles among us in the flesh, and His death tears the veil, opening the way into God's presence forever.

context.literary: This chapter describes the tabernacle from the inside out and bottom to top: the innermost linen curtains bearing cherubim, then the protective outer coverings, then the wooden frames giving the whole structure form, ending with the veil that divides the sanctuary into two unequal chambers.
context.historical: As a portable tent structure, the tabernacle was designed to travel with a nomadic people through the wilderness, unlike the fixed temple Solomon would later build; its materials, acacia wood, gold, and woven fabrics, were suited to both durability and mobility.
context.canonical: Hebrews 9 draws directly on this chapter's two chamber structure to explain the meaning of Christ's high priestly work, while Revelation's vision of the heavenly temple echoes and transcends this earthly pattern.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record God's actual pattern for Israel's portable sanctuary, showing through its careful, graded structure both the seriousness of approaching a holy God and His enduring desire to dwell among His people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a historical description of Israel's specific old covenant sanctuary, not a prescriptive pattern for church buildings today. Its abiding truth is the reality of the barrier sin creates between people and God, and that barrier's removal in Christ.

debates.question: How literally should later interpreters press the exact measurements and materials of the tabernacle for theological meaning?

views.label: Focus on the broad structure
views.case: Many interpreters caution against extracting detailed symbolic meaning from every measurement, cubit count, or clasp, since Scripture explains only some elements, such as the veil's function, and does not gloss most of the specific numbers.

views.label: Attention to what Hebrews itself highlights
views.case: Others note that the New Testament, especially Hebrews 9, does draw real theological weight from this chapter's basic two chamber structure and the veil specifically, suggesting the major architectural features, though not every measurement, do carry intended meaning.

debates.bearing: Both approaches affirm the central point: a graded structure kept sinful people at a distance from God's presence, and that distance is what Christ has decisively closed.

study.originalLanguages: The mishkan is God's own dwelling place among His people, moving as they move through the wilderness. Deep within it hangs the parokhet, the veil, embroidered with cherubim like the guardians of Eden, marking off the Most Holy Place where the ark and mercy seat rest, a boundary no ordinary Israelite, and even the high priest only once a year, could cross.

typology.type: The tabernacle, God's dwelling place among His people
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the incarnation, when the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, John 1:14.

typology.type: The veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place
typology.fulfillment: Torn in two at Christ's death, opening a new and living way into God's presence, Matthew 27:51, Hebrews 10:19-20.

study.christ: John deliberately echoes this chapter when he writes that the Word became flesh and 'tabernacled,' or dwelt, among us, John 1:14, presenting Jesus as the true meeting place between God and humanity. At the moment of His death, the temple veil, the very structure described here, was torn from top to bottom, showing that Christ's sacrifice has ended the separation this tabernacle was built to maintain, Hebrews 9:11-12.
study.god: God's careful, graded instructions show how seriously He takes His own holiness, keeping sinful people at a measured distance through curtain after curtain. Yet the entire structure exists because God wants to dwell among His people at all, a mercy visible even in its restrictions.

crossReferences.note: The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, using the same imagery as this chapter.
The temple veil is torn in two from top to bottom at the moment of Christ's death.
Explains the two chamber structure of the tabernacle and its meaning for approaching God.
Believers now have confidence to enter the Most Holy Place through the new and living way opened by Christ's body.

study.oneStory: God gives Israel a dwelling built in careful layers, keeping sinful people at a measured distance from His holy presence behind a final veil. That whole arrangement finds its answer in Jesus, who tabernacles among us in human flesh and, through His death, tears the veil, opening permanent access into the presence of God.

quiz.q: What was worked into the innermost linen curtains of the tabernacle?

options.text: Cherubim, skillfully worked into blue, purple, and scarlet yarn.
options.note: Verse 1 describes the ten inner curtains this way.

options.text: Images of the twelve tribes of Israel.
options.note: The tribal names appear later on the priestly breastpiece and shoulder stones, not on these curtains.

options.text: Plain, undecorated white linen only.
options.note: The curtains were richly woven with colored yarn and cherubim, not left plain.

options.text: No decoration was included.
options.note: Verse 1 explicitly describes cherubim skillfully worked into the curtains.

quiz.q: What materials made up the outer coverings that protected the tabernacle?

options.text: Goat hair curtains, then a covering of ram skins dyed red, then fine leather.
options.note: Verses 7 and 14 describe this layered outer protection.

options.text: Solid bronze plates.
options.note: Bronze was used for bases and utensils, not as a covering layer over the tent.

options.text: A single layer of undyed wool.
options.note: The coverings were specifically goat hair, ram skins dyed red, and fine leather, not undyed wool.

options.text: No outer covering was specified.
options.note: Verses 7 through 14 explicitly describe the layered outer coverings.

quiz.q: What material and construction were the tabernacle's upright frames made of?

options.text: Acacia wood overlaid with gold, standing on silver bases.
options.note: Verses 15, 19, and 29 describe this construction.

options.text: Solid stone blocks.
options.note: The frames were wood overlaid with gold, not stone.

options.text: Bronze frames on wooden bases.
options.note: The frames themselves were acacia wood overlaid with gold, resting on silver, not bronze, bases.

options.text: The frames' material is not described.
options.note: Verses 15 through 29 explicitly describe the frames' wood, gold overlay, and silver bases.

quiz.q: What did the veil separate inside the tabernacle?

options.text: The Holy Place from the Most Holy Place.
options.note: Verse 33 states this exact function of the veil.

options.text: The courtyard from the entrance gate.
options.note: That boundary was marked by a separate gate curtain, not the inner veil described here.

options.text: The table from the lampstand.
options.note: The table and lampstand stood on opposite sides of the same Holy Place, both outside the veil.

options.text: The veil served no dividing function.
options.note: Verse 33 explicitly states the veil separates the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place.

quiz.q: Where was the ark of the Testimony placed in relation to the veil?

options.text: Behind the veil, in the Most Holy Place.
options.note: Verse 33 places the ark of the Testimony behind the veil.

options.text: In front of the veil, in the Holy Place.
options.note: The table and lampstand stood in front of the veil; the ark itself was placed behind it.

options.text: Outside the tabernacle entirely.
options.note: The ark was the central furnishing kept inside, in the innermost chamber, not outside the structure.

options.text: Its placement is not specified.
options.note: Verse 33 explicitly places the ark of the Testimony behind the veil.

questions: Every layer of the tabernacle, from cherubim woven curtains to plain outer leather, moved from costly nearest God's presence to plain on the outside. Where in your own life is there a gap between how you appear outwardly and what is closest to your heart?
The veil kept even faithful Israelites from entering God's immediate presence. How often do you take for granted the access to God that Christ's torn veil has given you?

The tabernacle was God's mishkan, His dwelling place moving with His people through the wilderness. How does it change your view of God to know He chooses to dwell among His people rather than remain distant?
John says the Word tabernacled among us in Jesus. How does seeing Christ as God's true dwelling place shape the way you think about His humanity and His nearness to you?

The veil was torn at the very moment Christ died. What would it look like this week to actually live as someone with full, confident access into God's presence, rather than someone still standing outside the curtain?

translationNotes.note: Cherubim skillfully worked into the curtains recalls the cherubim guarding the way to the tree of life in Genesis 3:24, linking the tabernacle's design to the guarded holiness lost at the fall.

According to the pattern shown you on the mountain repeats language from 25:9 and 25:40, emphasizing that Moses was not designing the tabernacle himself but faithfully reproducing a pattern he was shown.

The veil, parokhet, is distinct from the outer entrance curtain in verse 36; the veil alone divides the Holy Place from the innermost Most Holy Place, and it is this veil that Matthew 27:51 records being torn at Christ's death.
